title: Tailwind CSS v4 installation and configuration with Vite
Radix UI Dialog with controlled state and animation
body:
Radix UI primitives are unstyled and accessible by default. A Dialog component with proper animation requires controlling open state and using CSS or Framer Motion for transitions. ```tsx import * as Dialog from '@radix-ui/react-dialog'; import { X } from 'lucide-react'; import { useState } from 'react'; export function ConfirmDialog({ onConfirm }: { onConfirm: () => void }) { const [open, setOpen] = useState(false); return ( Are you sure? This action cannot be undone.
); } ``` Always include `Dialog.Title` and `Dialog.Description` for screen readers. The `asChild` prop merges Radix behavior onto your own button elements instead of wrapping them in extra DOM nodes.

Radix UI Popover z-index stacking issues with portals
body:
Radix UI portals render content at the end of `document.body`, which can cause z-index stacking context problems when used inside modals or other layered UI.

**The problem:**
```tsx
// A popover inside a dialog — popover appears BEHIND the dialog overlay
<Dialog.Root>
  <Dialog.Portal>
    <Dialog.Overlay className="fixed inset-0 z-50 bg-black/50" />
    <Dialog.Content className="fixed z-50 ...">
      <Popover.Root>
        <Popover.Trigger>Open</Popover.Trigger>
        <Popover.Portal>
          {/* This renders at body root, z-index context is lost */}
          <Popover.Content className="z-50">
            I'm behind the dialog!
          </Popover.Content>
        </Popover.Portal>
      </Popover.Root>
    </Dialog.Content>
  </Dialog.Portal>
</Dialog.Root>
```

**Fix — use `container` prop or remove inner Portal:**
```tsx
<Popover.Portal container={dialogContentRef.current}>
  <Popover.Content className="z-[60]" sideOffset={5}>
    Now I'm correctly layered!
  </Popover.Content>
</Popover.Portal>

// Or simply remove the Portal for nested cases:
<Popover.Content className="z-[60]" sideOffset={5}>
  No portal needed inside a dialog
</Popover.Content>
```

The `container` prop on `Portal` controls where the portal element is appended. Setting it to the dialog content ref ensures proper stacking. Alternatively, removing `Popover.Portal` entirely keeps the content in the DOM tree of the dialog.

Container queries with Tailwind CSS v4 for component-level responsiveness
body:
CSS container queries let components respond to their container's size rather than the viewport. Tailwind v4 supports them natively. ```tsx export function Card({ title, description }: { title: string; description: string }) { return (

{title}

{description}

); } ``` The `@container` class establishes a containment context. Use `@sm:`, `@md:`, `@lg:` etc. as responsive prefixes scoped to the container width. **Named containers** for nested containment: ```tsx

Responsive to both containers

``` Container query breakpoints in Tailwind v4: `@xs` (20rem), `@sm` (24rem), `@md` (28rem), `@lg` (32rem), `@xl` (36rem), `@2xl` (42rem). These differ from viewport breakpoints. Container queries eliminate the need for prop-based responsive logic and work perfectly in reusable component libraries.

When to use Radix UI vs headless UI vs building custom accessible components
body:
Choosing between Radix UI, Headless UI, and custom components depends on your framework, design system maturity, and accessibility requirements.

**Radix UI** — best for React-only projects needing maximum flexibility:
```tsx
import * as Select from '@radix-ui/react-select';
// 30+ primitives, each independently installable
// npm install @radix-ui/react-select
<Select.Root>
  <Select.Trigger><Select.Value placeholder="Pick one" /></Select.Trigger>
  <Select.Portal>
    <Select.Content>
      <Select.Item value="a"><Select.ItemText>Option A</Select.ItemText></Select.Item>
    </Select.Content>
  </Select.Portal>
</Select.Root>
```

**Headless UI** — best for Tailwind CSS projects (by the Tailwind team):
```tsx
import { Listbox } from '@headlessui/react';
// Fewer primitives but tighter Tailwind integration
// Supports React and Vue
<Listbox value={selected} onChange={setSelected}>
  <Listbox.Button>{selected.name}</Listbox.Button>
  <Listbox.Options>
    <Listbox.Option value={option}>{option.name}</Listbox.Option>
  </Listbox.Options>
</Listbox>
```

**Custom components** — only when you need behavior no library provides. You must implement: focus management, keyboard navigation, ARIA attributes, screen reader announcements, and roving tabindex. This is 10-20x more work than using a primitive library.

**Recommendation:** Use Radix for complex design systems (shadcn/ui is built on it). Use Headless UI for simpler Tailwind projects. Never build custom unless you have dedicated accessibility QA.

title: CSS Grid responsive layout patterns with Tailwind CSS
body:
Common CSS Grid patterns in Tailwind for dashboards, galleries, and holy-grail layouts. **Auto-fill responsive grid (no media queries):** ```tsx
{cards.map(card => )}
``` **Dashboard layout with named areas:** ```tsx
Header
Sidebar
{children}
``` **Masonry-like with grid-rows auto:** ```tsx
{items.map(item => (
{item.content}
))}
``` **Subgrid for aligned nested grids (Tailwind v4):** ```tsx
Aligned col 1
Aligned col 2
Aligned col 3
``` The `auto-fill` + `minmax` pattern is the most versatile — items automatically wrap to new rows and stretch to fill available space with no breakpoint classes needed.

shadcn/ui component import errors after Next.js App Router migration
body:
When migrating from Pages Router to App Router with shadcn/ui, components break because many use React hooks internally and need the `'use client'` directive.

**Error:**
```
Error: useState only works in Client Components. Add the "use client" directive at the top of the file.
  at Button (./components/ui/button.tsx)
```

**The issue:** shadcn/ui components like `Dialog`, `DropdownMenu`, `Popover`, and `Tabs` use React state internally. In the App Router, all components are Server Components by default.

**Fix — add `'use client'` to interactive shadcn components:**
```tsx
// components/ui/dialog.tsx
'use client';  // ADD THIS LINE

import * as DialogPrimitive from '@radix-ui/react-dialog';
// ... rest of component
```

**Components that need `'use client'`:**
- Dialog, AlertDialog, Sheet
- DropdownMenu, ContextMenu, Menubar
- Popover, Tooltip, HoverCard
- Tabs, Accordion, Collapsible
- Select, Combobox, Command
- Toast, Sonner

**Components that DON'T need it:**
- Button (unless using onClick handlers)
- Badge, Card, Separator
- Avatar, AspectRatio
- Table, Skeleton

When you run `npx shadcn@latest add dialog`, the CLI now auto-adds `'use client'` for App Router projects. If you installed components before this update, add the directive manually.

Radix UI vs Ark UI vs React Aria: headless component library comparison
body:
Three major headless component libraries for React, each with different design philosophies.

**Radix UI** — compound components with portals:
```tsx
import * as DropdownMenu from '@radix-ui/react-dropdown-menu';
<DropdownMenu.Root>
  <DropdownMenu.Trigger asChild><button>Menu</button></DropdownMenu.Trigger>
  <DropdownMenu.Portal>
    <DropdownMenu.Content sideOffset={5}>
      <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={() => {}}>Edit</DropdownMenu.Item>
      <DropdownMenu.Separator />
      <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={() => {}}>Delete</DropdownMenu.Item>
    </DropdownMenu.Content>
  </DropdownMenu.Portal>
</DropdownMenu.Root>
```

**Ark UI** — state machine-based (by Chakra team):
```tsx
import { Menu } from '@ark-ui/react';
<Menu.Root>
  <Menu.Trigger>Menu</Menu.Trigger>
  <Menu.Positioner>
    <Menu.Content>
      <Menu.Item value="edit">Edit</Menu.Item>
      <Menu.Separator />
      <Menu.Item value="delete">Delete</Menu.Item>
    </Menu.Content>
  </Menu.Positioner>
</Menu.Root>
```

**React Aria** — hooks-based (by Adobe):
```tsx
import { useMenuTrigger, useMenu, useMenuItem } from 'react-aria';
import { useMenuTriggerState, useTreeState } from 'react-stately';
// Requires wiring up ~5 hooks manually per component
```

**Verdict:** Radix has the best ecosystem (shadcn/ui). Ark UI supports React, Vue, and Solid. React Aria has the most thorough accessibility implementation but requires the most code. For most React projects, Radix is the pragmatic choice. For multi-framework design systems, consider Ark UI.

shadcn/ui CLI commands and component customization workflow
body:
shadcn/ui is not a component library — it's a code generator. Components are copied into your project for full ownership and customization.

**Initialize in your project:**
```bash
npx shadcn@latest init
```

This creates `components.json` with your configuration:
```json
{
  "$schema": "https://ui.shadcn.com/schema.json",
  "style": "new-york",
  "rsc": true,
  "tsx": true,
  "tailwind": {
    "config": "",
    "css": "app/globals.css",
    "baseColor": "zinc"
  },
  "aliases": {
    "components": "@/components",
    "utils": "@/lib/utils",
    "ui": "@/components/ui",
    "hooks": "@/hooks"
  }
}
```

**Add components:**
```bash
npx shadcn@latest add button dialog card
npx shadcn@latest add -a  # Add ALL components
```

**Diff to see upstream changes:**
```bash
npx shadcn@latest diff button
```

**Customization workflow:**
1. Add component: `npx shadcn@latest add data-table`
2. Component appears in `components/ui/data-table.tsx`
3. Modify the file directly — it's your code now
4. Periodically run `diff` to see if upstream improved anything
5. Cherry-pick changes you want

Components depend on: `tailwind-merge`, `clsx`, `class-variance-authority`, `lucide-react`, and various `@radix-ui/*` packages. The CLI automatically installs required dependencies when adding a component.
